series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] mm: swap: bypass synchronous readahead unless the target needs I/O
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's swap readahead behavior to bypass synchronous readahead unless the target needs I/O. This change is made to improve performance in certain scenarios, such as when the faulting target is also served synchronously from a device. The patch introduces a trade-off between initial fault latency and throughput gain, with results showing improved build times and reduced swap readahead for zswap. The changes are made to the mm/swap_state.c file, specifically in the swap_cluster_readahead and swap_vma_readahead functions.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's swap handling to treat devices marked as SWP_SYNCHRONOUS_IO (such as zram) as synchronous in readahead operations. This change allows for better overlap of I/O operations between disk and zram, improving performance in mixed workloads. The modification extends the swap_entry_synchronous() function to take into account the swap device and returns true for entries on SWP_SYNCHRONOUS_IO devices, ensuring that these reads are deferred until the second pass. This patch is part of a series aimed at optimizing swap handling and has been tested with positive results in mixed zram+disk workloads.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: swap: overlap synchronous readahead decompression with disk I/O

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] mm: swap: overlap synchronous readahead decompression with disk I/O

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the swap readahead process by overlapping synchronous decompression of zswap-resident entries with disk I/O operations. The current implementation serializes CPU work in front of disk reads, introducing unnecessary latency. To address this, the patch splits the window walk into two passes: one issues asynchronous device reads and defers synchronous neighbors, while the second pass reads deferred entries concurrently with ongoing I/O operations. This approach reduces added latency and improves swap-in performance.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to optimize swap readahead for zswap by splitting the window walk into two passes and reordering it around the distinction between synchronous and asynchronous device reads. The first pass overlaps the synchronous work with disk reads, while the second pass is skipped when the target needs no I/O. This approach helps mixed workloads (zswap + disk) and keeps synchronous-target page-fault latency low.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/2] mm: zswap: free synchronous-IO writeback folios directly
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the zswap writeback process by freeing synchronous-IO swap device folios directly after writing, rather than leaving them on the LRU for page reclaim to handle later. This reduces memory residency time and avoids unnecessary reclaim scans. The change is specific to synchronous-IO devices, as asynchronous and filesystem-backed swaps complete in interrupt context and are handled separately.

and swapped out again. We probably shouldn't try to remove_mapping()
such a folio?

(filemap_end_dropbehind()).

folio_test_active() and folio_mapped(), but I must admit - I do not
remember why I did it. Could just be me making sure we dont get rid of
frequently used folio rather than for strict correctness. :)

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a swapin has claimed the folio before it is freed, and requested additional checks to ensure the folio is not reclaimed prematurely.

Reviewer Alexandre Ghiti noted that the writeback check in the patch is not necessary because dropbehind folios are allocated off LRU, making it impossible for a racing swapin to reclaim and swap out the folio while under writeback. However, he also suggested adding the check as a precautionary measure, considering other potential 'swap triggers' like MADV_PAGEOUT.

Reviewer Alexandre Ghiti pointed out that folio_mapped() is unnecessary because remove_mapping() checks the refcount, and also noted that folio_test_active() will always be false when allocated off-lru, requesting confirmation before checking for it in writeback paths.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Nhat,

On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 5:56 PM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:

---

Actually it took me some time to eliminate the writeback check, I
think it is not necessary.

First, we only call remove_mapping() at the end of writeback (easy scenario).

Then comes your scenario, the hard one. But it cannot happen because
dropbehind folios are allocated off lru. Which means that a racing
swapin can re-dirty the folio (that will get caught by remove_mapping
itself) but it cannot be swapped out (ie under writeback) because it
is off lru and then cannot be reclaimed.

But writing that, I realize I may have missed other "swap triggers"
(MADV_PAGEOUT for example). Perhaps adding the check is safer (and
cheap anyway), what do you think?

---

folio_mapped() is not necessary because remove_mapping() checks the
refcount. folio_test_active() is always false in my case since
allocated off-lru.

So unless someone confirms what I said above for writeback, I'll check
for it, it's cheap anyway.

Thanks,

Alex

review_comments.author: Barry Song
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Barry Song noted that the patch does not provide an equivalent mechanism to reclaim folios directly like MGLRU does, and requested a similar approach for synchronous-IO writeback

Reviewer Barry Song noted that asynchronous swap devices do not free memory immediately and suggested freeing anonymous folios directly after writeout to avoid occupying memory until another reclaim scan

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a concurrent swapin has claimed the folio, and requested that the lock be dropped before calling try_to_unmap()
review_comments.raw_body:
With MGLRU, we already have code like the one below that reclaims
folios directly without requiring another reclaim scan, whereas the
active/inactive LRU path doesn't have an equivalent mechanism.

                /* retry folios that may have missed
folio_rotate_reclaimable() */
                if (!skip_retry && !folio_test_active(folio) &&
!folio_mapped(folio) &&
                    !folio_test_dirty(folio) && !folio_test_writeback(folio)) {
                        list_move(&folio->lru, &clean);
                        continue;
                }

---

Might be a bit off-topic, but I've been thinking about this problem
for a while.

MADV_PAGEOUT, and reclaim in general, don't actually free memory
when using asynchronous swap devices. Do you think it would make
sense to free the folio directly, at least for anonymous folios,
once it has been written out, instead of waiting for another reclaim
scan?

It seems a bit odd that after MADV_PAGEOUT completes, the memory is
still occupied.

---

Best Regards
Barry

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Kairui Song suggested limiting the immediate drop of swap cache folios to specific cases like MADV_PAGEOUT and zswap cold writeback, as it can help catch fast refaults for common swap, but is not necessary for direct synchronous IO devices or zswap compression.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes but I think that immediate drop might be actually better be
limited to things like MADV_PAGEOUT and zswap cold writeback? For
common swap, keeping the swap cache after writeback sometimes helps a
lot by catching fast refaults. Because we perform swap writeback
asynchronously, we can easily overshoot the reclaim and send more
folios into writeback than necessary.

For direct synchronous IO devices or direct zswap compression that's
not a problem because their writeback is synchronized, so the
reclaimer waits. And we already free them immediately after writeback.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/2] mm: zswap: deferred dropbehind free of writeback folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of writeback folios in the zswap compression algorithm by deferring their free operation to a workqueue that runs after writeback completion, ensuring they are promptly freed even for asynchronous block devices and filesystem-backed swap. This prevents cold folios from being left on the LRU for reclaim to clean up later. The patch modifies the `folio_end_writeback` function to check if the folio is off-LRU due to zswap writeback and, if so, defers its free operation using a new function `zswap_writeback_dropbehind_folio`. This approach ensures that zswap writeback folios are handled consistently across different I/O types.

Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested batching the free step of zswap writeback folios after remove_mapping() succeeds for each folio, proposing to add the folios to a batch and then call folios_put_refs() in the entire batch.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch improves the performance of zswap by freeing cold writeback folios promptly after writeback completion, reducing unnecessary reclaim scans and keeping memory resident for shorter periods. The patch consists of two parts: one for synchronous-IO devices (zram, brd, pmem) that can free the folio directly in the calling context, and another for asynchronous block devices and filesystem-backed swap that allocate the folio off the LRU and defer the free to a workqueue. The results show significant improvements in performance metrics such as page rotations, steals, scans, and build times, with most folios being freed promptly on writeback completion.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yosry Ahmed noted that the implementation is too specific to zswap and suggested adding general support for swap-backed pages in folio_end_dropbehind() before having zswap opt into it, also pointing out a challenge of handling interrupt context.
review_comments.raw_body:
I took a quick look through the patches and the implementation seems
too zswap-specific.

I am not super familiar with how the drop-behind mechanism works in
general, but it seems like it's currently intended for file pages and
not swap-backed pages. From a high-level perspective, it seems like we
should add support for swap dropbehind in general first (regardless of
zswap), e.g. by making folio_end_dropbehind() properly handle them,
and then have zswap opt into this?

I guess one of the challenges is that folio_end_dropbehind() only
frees folios in task context while we need to support interrupt
context, but this also doesn't seem like something that should be
handled in zswap.

In theory, there may be a use case for doing dropbehind for swap in
general, right?

patches_acked.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/7] btrfs: some optimizations and cleanups for log syncing

patches_submitted.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/8] dax/kmem: support for private NUMA nodes

version_history.subject: [RFC PATCH 0/8] dax/kmem: support for private NUMA nodes

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 1/8] dax/kmem: add dax_file= to expose an anonymous-fault char device
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a 'dax_file' knob to the dax/kmem driver, which exposes an anonymous-fault char device when set. The device allows mapping memory ranges as ordinary ANONYMOUS mappings bound to the device's node, useful for private nodes where faults are limited to the device's memory ranges. The mapping is not S_DAX, and a device-lifetime mempolicy is built at online time to keep the mapping pinned to the node. The patch also includes changes to handle mmap operations and install the mempolicy.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 2/8] dax/kmem: add private= to online memory as an N_MEMORY_PRIVATE node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a 'private' knob to dax/kmem, allowing memory to be onlined as an N_MEMORY_PRIVATE node. This marks the node private and excludes it from every fallback zonelist. The device can only be made private when in an unplugged state. A private node is solely accessible via mmap on the dax character device and has its dax-file mappings default to base-page faults for deterministic allocation and failure handling. Capabilities are added as part of this patch, but no capabilities are actually set until later commits.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 3/8] dax/kmem: add the reclaim opt-in (CAP_RECLAIM) for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability for private kmem nodes to opt-in to reclaim, which allows them to recover THP (Transparent Huge Pages) compatibility. The opt-in is done through a new attribute called 'reclaim' that can be enabled or disabled. This feature is intended to address issues where high-order allocations fail despite available memory. The patch introduces a helper function KMEM_PRIVATE_CAP_ATTR() for future use and adds the necessary code to enable the reclaim opt-in.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 4/8] dax/kmem: add the user_numa opt-in (CAP_USER_NUMA) for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an opt-in feature called 'user_numa' that allows userspace to control memory residency on a private node via mempolicy and migration. The feature is enabled by setting the CAP_USER_NUMA capability, which requires administrative privileges. This change provides more fine-grained control over memory management for users with specific needs.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 5/8] dax/kmem: add the hotunplug opt-in (CAP_HOTUNPLUG) for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability for private nodes to opt-in to hotunplug, allowing their memory to be bulk-evacuated when a device is removed. This feature is controlled by the CAP_HOTUNPLUG flag and can be toggled through sysfs attributes. The goal of this change is to improve system manageability and flexibility in scenarios where devices need to be removed or replaced.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 6/8] dax/kmem: add the demotion and numa_balancing opt-ins for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new sysfs opt-ins for private nodes in the Linux kernel: 'demotion' and 'numa_balancing'. The 'demotion' option requires reclaim to be enabled, which is enforced at hotplug by node_private_register(). This allows administrators to control how memory is allocated on a per-device basis.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 7/8] dax/kmem: add the ltpin opt-in (CAP_LTPIN) for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds an opt-in setting called 'ltpin' for private nodes in the Linux kernel's DAX (Direct Access) subsystem. The ltpin setting allows long-term GUP pins to target the node's folios, which is necessary because these pins block migration and hot-unplug indefinitely. Without this opt-in, such pins would be refused on a private node unless it explicitly enables the 'ltpin' feature.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH 8/8] dax/kmem: add dax/adistance to make private node adistance configurable
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to configure the abstract distance of a private node in the tiering hierarchy, allowing for adjustments to be made dynamically without requiring static configuration data. The patch introduces a new field 'adistance' to the dax_kmem_data structure and provides functions to set and get this value. This feature is intended for testing purposes, where it is useful to move nodes to different memory tiers without affecting other hotplugged memory.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for private NUMA nodes to the dax/kmem driver, allowing for more fine-grained control over memory allocation and management. The patches introduce new uapi interfaces for controlling private node settings, including options for reclaiming memory, enabling user-level NUMA policies, and configuring adistance for private nodes. The goal is to enable testing of private node behaviors and determine whether some of these features are useful for coherent memory devices. The series includes 8 patches that modify the dax/kmem driver to expose new interfaces and capabilities.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 36/36] KVM: selftests: add a guest_memfd FLAG_BIND_NODE test

version_history.subject: [PATCH v5 36/36] KVM: selftests: add a guest_memfd FLAG_BIND_NODE test

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a standalone test to verify the functionality of GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_BIND_NODE, which allows a guest_memfd to be bound to a specific NUMA node. The test creates a guest_memfd bound to a given node, maps and faults it from the host, and checks that every folio landed on the requested node using move_pages(2). It also covers rejection paths for invalid inputs such as nonzero pad, out-of-range nodes, and missing flags. The test requires CONFIG_NUMA and uses a default (non-protected) VM to allow the host to fault the fd and observe placement.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v5 00/36] Private Memory NUMA Nodes

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/36] mm: refactor find_next_best_node to find_next_best_node_in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the find_next_best_node function to make it more flexible by allowing it to take an explicit list of candidates instead of just the N_MEMORY set. This change enables building fallback lists that include non-N_MEMORY nodes, but does not introduce any functional changes as all current callers still select from N_MEMORY. The refactor involves renaming the function to find_next_best_node_in and updating several calls in memory-tiers.c and page_alloc.c to use the new function.

review_comments.author: Balbir Singh
review_comments.summary: Gave Acked-by

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/36] mm/page_alloc: refactor build_node_zonelist() out of build_zonelists()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the build_node_zonelist() function out of build_zonelists(). The new function builds a per-node fallback list from a candidate nodemask, allowing zonelists to be built from these lists instead of just the default N_MEMORY node state list. This change does not introduce any functional changes and maintains the same behavior as before. The patch also updates various functions to use the new build_node_zonelist() function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/36] mm/page_alloc: let the bulk and folio allocators carry alloc_flags
series_items.patch_summary: This patch allows the bulk and folio allocators to carry alloc_flags, which enables callers to select allocator behavior such as an alternate zonelist. The patch adds new functions in page_alloc.h that take an explicit alloc_flags parameter, matching the existing __alloc_pages_noprof function. The public gfp.h wrappers remain flag-free. This change does not introduce any functional changes, as all callers currently pass ALLOC_DEFAULT.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/36] numa: introduce N_MEMORY_PRIVATE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new node type called N_MEMORY_PRIVATE to support devices that want to hotplug memory onto a node without exposing it for general consumption. The patch adds infrastructure for private nodes, including a per-node container, functions to check if a page resides on a private node or is managed by a zone device, and registration API for private nodes. It also updates sysfs attributes and helpers to handle private nodes correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/36] mm: add ZONELIST_PRIVATE(_NOFALLBACK) for N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new zonelist isolation mechanism to prevent unintentional allocations from reaching N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes. It introduces two new zonelists: ZONELIST_PRIVATE and ZONELIST_PRIVATE_NOFALLBACK, which are used for private node allocations. The patch also adds explicit alloc() functions for private node allocations and modifies the allocator's behavior to use these new zonelists. This change aims to make N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes effectively invisible to normal callers of page_alloc, while still allowing intentional private node allocations.

review_comments.author: Richard Cheng
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Richard Cheng questioned whether ZONELIST_PRIVATE should use an explicit target nodemask to prevent fallback to another owner's private node, potentially violating node isolation.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Gregory,

A question about this part, if my understanding is right,
ZONELIST_PRIVATE contains all private nodes, should we use some explicit target
nodemask here to make sure only the targeting owner's private node can be touched?

Otherwise it might fall back into a different owner's private node.

--Richard

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that private nodes cannot fall back to other private nodes in the current design and suggests waiting for a specific use case before extending zonelist construction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/36] cpuset: exclude private nodes from cpuset.mems (default-open)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's cpuset functionality to exclude private nodes from being managed by cpusets. Private nodes are treated as globally accessible resources, rather than being partitioned among cpusets. The patch also ensures that memory policies are re-bound when a private node leaves N_MEMORY_PRIVATE state on hot-unplug. This change aims to prevent issues with silent background operations and migrations due to cpuset membership changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/36] mm/memory_hotplug: disallow migration-driven private node hotunplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the hotunplug of private node memory that is being migrated, as this can lead to unsafe conditions. Instead of attempting to hotunplug the memory, it fails the operation and requires the owner to safely drain the folios before proceeding. This change breaks out of the hotunplug attempt entirely when a fatal migration failure occurs, rather than spinning indefinitely on a failed migration attempt.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/36] mm/mempolicy: skip private node folios when queueing for migration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy module to skip private node folios when queueing them for migration. Private nodes are not included in policy nodemasks, but could still be encountered during an mbind(MPOL_MF_MOVE) walk. The patch adds a filter to prevent mempolicy-driven migration from moving private-node memory, similar to the existing filter for ZONE_DEVICE folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/36] mm/migrate: disallow userland driven migration for private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents userland-driven migration of memory on private nodes by adding a check to the migration code. The change uses the same filter locations as zone device folios, but instead checks for private managed memory. This ensures that sensitive data on private nodes is not accidentally migrated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/36] mm/madvise: disallow madvise operations on private node folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents madvise operations from being performed on private node memory by adding filters to the huge pages and zone device locations. It does this by checking if a folio is part of a private node before allowing madvise operations, effectively disallowing these operations on such memory. This change aims to prevent potential issues that could arise from madvise operations on private node memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/36] mm/compaction: disallow compaction on private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents compaction from occurring on private nodes in the Linux kernel's memory management system. Compaction requires the ability to migrate pages, which is not yet supported on private nodes. The patch adds checks to skip compaction on private nodes in several functions related to compaction and node management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 12/36] mm/page_alloc: clear private node watermarks and system reserves
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation code to exclude private nodes from system reserve calculations and set their watermarks to zero. Private nodes are not supported for reclaim, making their watermarks pointless. Including them in system reserve calculations can dilute DRAM watermarks and lead to poor OOM kill behavior. The changes aim to improve the OOM killer's ability to resolve general system pressure by releasing private node capacity.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 13/36] mm/mempolicy: disallow NUMA Balancing prot_none on private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory policy to prevent NUMA balancing on private nodes, treating them similarly to zone device memory. This is because private nodes do not currently support migration. The change affects how folios are mapped for protection.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 14/36] mm/damon: skip private node memory in DAMON migration and pageout
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the DAMON (Data Access Monitor) kernel module to skip private node memory during migration and pageout operations. Private node memory is not reclaimable by default, so this change ensures that DAMON does not attempt to migrate or reclaim such memory. The modification involves adding checks for private node memory in both the migration and pageout paths of the DAMON code.

review_comments.author: SJ Park
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park questioned whether private-node folios are truly reclaimable by default and requested a check to confirm this; they also asked about the potential consequences of attempting to page out private node memory.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle private node memory correctly, as it is not reclaimable by default and migration to such nodes is also unsupported.
review_comments.raw_body:
"by default".  Does that mean it could be reclaimable in some situations?  If
so, could we check if it is reclaimable?

Also, what happens if we just try paging out the private node memory?  Will it
simply fail?  Or, make some problems?

---

Thanks,
SJ

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that private node memory is not reclaimable by default and explained why it's skipped in DAMON migration and pageout paths, no fix planned

Author acknowledged that private node folios are handled similarly to ZONE_DEVICE folios, but expressed uncertainty about DAMON's handling of zone device pages.

Author acknowledged that migration operations to private nodes are allowed, but explained that operations on private node folios are refused unless a capability bit is set.
review_comments.raw_body:
See the damon changes in:

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/al-pkvmgIxGu3LzM@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/T/#mfb99303c85dd9d4c3f58832dc28fc55583a7217a

---

user controls for MADV_ commands that affect private node folios would
skip those folios - exactly same as ZONE_DEVICE folios.

Which I'm not sure damon actually handles correctly presently (zone
device).  I remember looking around damon and thinking to myself "what
if this is a zone device page?" and not being able to find a satisfying
answer.

---

Technically there is nothing in migration core to prevent migration
operations, it's done on a service basis - hotunplug, reclaim/demotion,
user numa (mbind, migrate/move_pages) etc.

Operations on private nodes/private node folioes are refused if the
capability bit is not set.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: SJ Park

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 15/36] mm/ksm: skip KSM for managed-memory folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the kernel's KSM (Kernel Samepage Merging) functionality to skip merging and scanning operations for private memory folios, which are intended for explicit placement. This change prevents KSM from operating on private node folios by default, thus maintaining the purpose of private memory. The modification uses a filter location similar to that used for zone_device folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 16/36] mm/khugepaged: skip private node folios when trying to collapse.
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the khugepaged functionality to skip private node folios when attempting to collapse huge pages. The collapse operation can cause new THP allocations and memory migrations, which are handled similarly to zone_device pages by disallowing collapse. This change aims to prevent potential issues arising from these operations on private node folios.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 17/36] mm/vmscan: disallow reclaim of private node memory
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the Linux kernel from attempting to reclaim memory on private nodes by adding a filter in the shrink_node function. Private nodes do not support reclaim by default, so this change ensures that reclaimers such as direct and kswapd do not operate on them. The approach taken is to check if a node is private at the common chokepoint for most reclaimers, preventing unnecessary work and potential issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 18/36] mm/gup: disallow longterm pin of private node folios
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents long-term pinning of folios on private nodes, which would lead to incorrect behavior in the GUP long-term path. It introduces a new function `folio_longterm_pin_forbidden()` that returns true for any private node folio and uses this function to reject such pins before migration. The gup-fast path is also updated to defer private folios to the slow path via `folio_allows_longterm_pin()`. This change ensures that private node folios are neither pinned nor migrated, preventing potential issues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 19/36] proc: include N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes in numa_maps output
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the numa_maps output in Linux to include private nodes that are not part of the N_MEMORY state. This is achieved by adding a check for N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes in the page-table walkers and modifying the show_numa_map function to print these nodes. The goal is to make private-node mappings visible in numa_maps, which was previously not possible due to filtering based on N_MEMORY.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 20/36] mm/memcontrol: account private-node memory in per-node stats
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's memory control system to account private-node memory in per-node statistics. Currently, private nodes are not included in per-node views of memory usage because their lruvec counters are not refreshed. This patch fixes this issue by including N_MEMORY_PRIVATE in the flush and display process, ensuring that private-node memory is correctly reported per node. The changes affect two files: mm/memcontrol-v1.c and mm/memcontrol.c. The patch is unconditional and does not depend on future capabilities.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 21/36] proc/kcore: include private-node RAM in the kcore RAM map
series_items.patch_summary: The patch modifies the kcore_ram_list function in fs/proc/kcore.c to include private-node RAM in the kcore RAM map, ensuring that all memory is exposed in /proc/kcore.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 22/36] mm/mempolicy: add MPOL_F_PRIVATE and zonelist selection
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, MPOL_F_PRIVATE, to the Linux memory policy system. This flag indicates that a policy contains a nodemask with private nodes and makes these nodes reachable via standard memory policies. The patch also introduces zonelist selection into the mpol allocator interfaces using the alloc_flags carried by page_alloc. With this change, a VMA can successfully service faults from private node memory, and cpuset rebinding does not affect private nodes. The patch is currently a no-op as nothing sets MPOL_F_PRIVATE yet, but it lays the groundwork for future use.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 23/36] mm/mempolicy: apply policy at the kernel zone for private-node binds
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a livelock issue in the Linux kernel's memory policy system by introducing a new function, `policy_private_has_kernel_zone`, which checks if any private node in a policy can satisfy non-movable allocations. The function is used to determine whether to apply the policy at the kernel zone for private-node binds. This change ensures that kernel allocations are not forced onto zones that cannot satisfy them, preventing infinite retries and improving system performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 24/36] mm/mempolicy: add in-kernel MPOL_BIND interfaces for drivers/services
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds two new interfaces to the Linux kernel's memory policy (mempolicy) module: mpol_bind_node() and mpol_private_bind(). These interfaces allow drivers or services to create a mempolicy that is pinned to a specific NUMA node, either publicly (mpol_bind_node()) or privately (mpol_private_bind()). The patch also updates the mpol_set_nodemask function to handle private nodes correctly. This change enables drivers to manage memory policies for VMAs they control, and provides a way for them to bypass certain security checks.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 25/36] mm/memory_hotplug: support N_MEMORY_PRIVATE node hotplug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for hotplugging memory onto a private node controlled by a module. It introduces a new function, add_private_memory_driver_managed(), which allows modules to register as owners of a private node and manage its memory. The patch also updates the common __add_memory_driver_managed() function to only allow in-tree modules to hotplug N_MEMORY nodes. Private nodes are opted out of starting kswapd/kcompactd by default. If the add fails, the registration is undone. Offline_pages() clears both N_MEMORY and N_MEMORY_PRIVATE flags, while offline_and_remove_memory_ranges() drops private node registration once the last node's memory is removed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 26/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_RECLAIM for opted-in private node reclaim
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a mechanism to opt private nodes into the reclaim process, allowing them to participate in memory reclamation operations such as kswapd and direct reclaim. The patch introduces a new capability called NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_RECLAIM that can be set by drivers for private nodes to enable or disable specific services like compaction, madvise, and DAMON. This allows for more fine-grained control over memory management on private nodes, preventing unpredictable behavior in certain workflows. The patch also updates various kernel modules to use the new capability and handle node-private reclaim accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 27/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_USER_NUMA for userland numa controls
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a mechanism to allow userland NUMA controls on private nodes in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_USER_NUMA, which allows userspace to place or migrate memory on private nodes. The patch also updates various functions and interfaces to enforce this capability, including mbind(), set_mempolicy(), move_pages(), and migrate_pages(). This change enables more fine-grained control over NUMA management in userland applications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 28/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_HOTUNPLUG for opted-in private nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for private nodes to be hot-unplugged by introducing a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_HOTUNPLUG. The capability allows memory_hotplug to migrate folios on private nodes for the purpose of hot-unplugging memory. Without this capability, hot-unplugging fails if any folio on the node is allocated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 29/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_DEMOTION for private-node tiering demotion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_DEMOTION, to allow private nodes to participate in the tiering demotion hierarchy. When set, memory-tiers will add the node to the demotion set and reclaim's demote path may target it. This requires CAP_RECLAIM to be enabled as well, which is enforced at registration. The patch also introduces a new function, node_allows_demotion(), to check if a private node allows demotion.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 30/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_NUMA_BALANCING for private-node NUMA balancing
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for private-node NUMA balancing in the Linux kernel. It introduces a new capability, NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_NUMA_BALANCING, which allows private nodes to participate in NUMA balancing. The patch also includes changes to the node_private.h and internal.h files to implement this new capability. Unlike demotion, NUMA balancing is not reclaim-driven, so this capability stands alone and does not require CAP_RECLAIM. This change enables access-based promotion/migration of private nodes, allowing them to participate in NUMA balancing like normal nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 31/36] mm: add NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_LTPIN for private node folio pinning
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the capability to opt-in private nodes for long-term GUP pinning, allowing them to be pinned like ordinary memory or migrated and pinned if in a movable zone. The change introduces a new flag NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_LTPIN and modifies existing functions to check for this capability. This enables more flexible handling of private node folios and allows for better utilization of system resources.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 32/36] mm/khugepaged: base private node collapse eligiblity on actor/cap bits
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces changes to the khugepaged daemon's behavior regarding private node collapse eligibility, based on actor/cap bits. The goal is to isolate private nodes from khugepaged and prevent silent collapse of private node folios. The patch also updates the MADV_COLLAPSE userland construct to be consistent with other userspace interfaces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 33/36] Documentation/mm: describe private (N_MEMORY_PRIVATE) memory nodes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation for private memory nodes, which are NUMA nodes whose memory is hotplugged by a driver and deliberately hidden from the kernel's normal memory management. The documentation covers the isolation model, ZONELIST_PRIVATE, driver provisioning API, capability opt-in model, and observability surfaces. Private memory nodes are marked as N_MEMORY_PRIVATE instead of N_MEMORY and are not considered by the page allocator's normal or fallback paths. This allows drivers to provide a block of NUMA-addressable memory that the rest of the kernel will not allocate from on its own.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 34/36] mm/mempolicy: add mpol_set_shared_policy_range()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function mpol_set_shared_policy_range() that installs an already-built policy over an arbitrary page range in the shared policy tree. The function takes as input the shared policy, start and end page offsets, and a fully-built policy. This allows for more flexibility when binding policies to backing inodes from in-kernel code. Additionally, mpol_set_shared_policy() is reimplemented as a thin wrapper that derives its range from a VMA, sharing an underlying path with the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 35/36] KVM: guest_memfd: bind backing memory to a NUMA node at creation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, GUEST_MEMFD_FLAG_BIND_NODE, to the KVM guest memory file descriptor (memfd) API. When this flag is set, the kernel binds the backing memory of the memfd to a specified NUMA node at creation time, ensuring that all allocated pages are on the same node without requiring userspace to use mbind(). The patch also adds support for this flag in the KVM_CAP_GUEST_MEMFD_FLAGS capability and updates the include files accordingly. This change is only enabled when CONFIG_NUMA is set.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch introduces the concept of 'Private Memory Nodes', which are isolated by default and can be opted into specific services such as page allocation and OOM killing. The goal is to flip the current dynamic where NUMA-hosted memory is accessible by default, and instead isolate it and then opt-in to specific services that the device says is safe. This allows for more secure and efficient use of accelerators, special memory devices, network devices, and other types of memory. The patch reduces complexity and introduces a dedicated zonelist for private nodes, making it easier to manage and control memory access.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the find_next_best_node() function, which was selecting nodes from the full N_MEMORY set without considering other candidates. The author refactored the function into find_next_best_node_in(), which takes an explicit candidates nodemask and allows building fallback lists with non-N_MEMORY candidates. No functional change is made, as every caller still selects from N_MEMORY.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about hotplugging memory onto a node without exposing it for general consumption by introducing N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes, which are mutually exclusive with N_MEMORY nodes. The author explained that this is necessary to re-use portions of mm/ services for management without exposing the memory for general consumption.

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed concerns about partitioning private nodes via cpuset and attempting to hotunplug device-backed private node memory without coordination. The author explained that isolating by cpuset offers no functionality and creates issues on rebind, so they decided not to partition private nodes via cpuset. They also stated that hotunplug should fail for private node memory until the owner drains the folios safely.
review_comments.raw_body:
N_MEMORY_PRIVATE node access is gated by per-node capabilities and
the zonelist.  Isolating by cpuset offers no functionality and
creates issues on rebind when a cpuset loses access to that node
(in particular: it generates migrations that may not be supported).

Do not partition private nodes via cpuset.mem, instead treat them
as globally accessible resources.  Do not engage in silent background
operations on these nodes due to cpuset.mem membership changing.

The result: cpuset operations checking for node validity always
allow N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes.

On hot-unplug, we still need to rebind memory policies if the
private node has left N_MEMORY_PRIVATE (i.e. no more memory).

Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
---
 kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c | 26 ++++++++++++++++++++++----
 mm/memcontrol.c        |  2 ++
 2 files changed, 24 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c b/kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c
index dfd0f827e3b92..05468f95c10bd 100644
--- a/kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c
+++ b/kernel/cgroup/cpuset.c
@@ -26,6 +26,7 @@
 #include <linux/mempolicy.h>
 #include <linux/mm.h>
 #include <linux/memory.h>
+#include <linux/node_private.h>
 #include <linux/rcupdate.h>
 #include <linux/sched.h>
 #include <linux/sched/deadline.h>
@@ -3867,7 +3868,8 @@ static void cpuset_handle_hotplug(void)
 	static DECLARE_WORK(hk_sd_work, hk_sd_workfn);
 	static cpumask_t new_cpus;
 	static nodemask_t new_mems;
-	bool cpus_updated, mems_updated;
+	static nodemask_t prev_priv_mems;
+	bool cpus_updated, mems_updated, priv_shrank;
 	bool on_dfl = is_in_v2_mode();
 	struct tmpmasks tmp, *ptmp = NULL;
 
@@ -3890,6 +3892,14 @@ static void cpuset_handle_hotplug(void)
 		       !cpumask_empty(subpartitions_cpus);
 	mems_updated = !nodes_equal(top_cpuset.effective_mems, new_mems);
 
+	/*
+	 * Private nodes are not partitioned by cpuset, but if one leaves
+	 * N_MEMORY_PRIVATE we still need to run mpol_rebind_* to clean up
+	 * mempolicies that are binding them.
+	 */
+	priv_shrank = !nodes_subset(prev_priv_mems, node_states[N_MEMORY_PRIVATE]);
+	prev_priv_mems = node_states[N_MEMORY_PRIVATE];
+
 	/* For v1, synchronize cpus_allowed to cpu_active_mask */
 	if (cpus_updated) {
 		cpuset_force_rebuild();
@@ -3922,9 +3932,12 @@ static void cpuset_handle_hotplug(void)
 			top_cpuset.mems_allowed = new_mems;
 		top_cpuset.effective_mems = new_mems;
 		spin_unlock_irq(&callback_lock);
-		cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask(&top_cpuset);
 	}
 
+	/* Rebind task mempolicies if any memory node changed state */
+	if (mems_updated || priv_shrank)
+		cpuset_update_tasks_nodemask(&top_cpuset);
+
 	mutex_unlock(&cpuset_mutex);
 
 	/* if cpus or mems changed, we need to propagate to descendants */
@@ -4155,11 +4168,13 @@ nodemask_t cpuset_mems_allowed(struct task_struct *tsk)
  * cpuset_nodemask_valid_mems_allowed - check nodemask vs. current mems_allowed
  * @nodemask: the nodemask to be checked
  *
- * Are any of the nodes in the nodemask allowed in current->mems_allowed?
+ * Are any of the nodes in the nodemask usable?  N_MEMORY nodes must be in
+ * current->mems_allowed, while N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes are always valid.
  */
 int cpuset_nodemask_valid_mems_allowed(const nodemask_t *nodemask)
 {
-	return nodes_intersects(*nodemask, current->mems_allowed);
+	return nodes_intersects(*nodemask, current->mems_allowed) ||
+	       nodes_intersects(*nodemask, node_states[N_MEMORY_PRIVATE]);
 }
 
 /*
@@ -4223,6 +4238,9 @@ bool cpuset_current_node_allowed(int node, gfp_t gfp_mask)
 
 	if (in_interrupt())
 		return true;
+	/* N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes are not partitioned by cpusets.mems */
+	if (node_is_private(node))
+		return true;
 	if (node_isset(node, current->mems_allowed))
 		return true;
 	/*
diff --git a/mm/memcontrol.c b/mm/memcontrol.c
index 8319ad8c5c23a..f0dde52dc9e0e 100644
--- a/mm/memcontrol.c
+++ b/mm/memcontrol.c
@@ -6069,6 +6069,8 @@ void mem_cgroup_node_filter_allowed(struct mem_cgroup *memcg, nodemask_t *mask)
 	 * mask is acceptable.
 	 */
 	cpuset_nodes_allowed(memcg->css.cgroup, &allowed);
+	/* N_MEMORY_PRIVATE nodes are not partitioned by cpuset, include them */
+	nodes_or(allowed, allowed, node_states[N_MEMORY_PRIVATE]);
 	nodes_and(*mask, *mask, allowed);
 }
 
-- 
2.53.0-Meta

---

By default, device-backed private node memory may not be safe to
migrate without coordination.

Do not attempt to hotunplug private node memory, instead fail the
hotunplug attempt, and require the owner to drain the folios
safely before allowing hotunplug to proceed.

Unlike transient / longterm pins causing failures, this is not a
transient condition - so we can break out of the hotunplug attempt
entirely instead of spinning forever on a failed migration attempt.

Signed-off-by: Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net>
---
 mm/memory_hotplug.c | 21 +++++++++++++++++----
 1 file changed, 17 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)

diff --git a/mm/memory_hotplug.c b/mm/memory_hotplug.c
index 226ab9cb078ad..2b9c0830821e6 100644
--- a/mm/memory_hotplug.c
+++ b/mm/memory_hotplug.c
@@ -34,6 +34,7 @@

nodes are allowed), but an mbind(MPOL_MF_MOVE) walk can still encounter a
private-node folio in the range.  Skip such folios so mempolicy-driven
migration never moves private-node memory.

We already do this for ZONE_DEVICE folios - use the same filter points
for ZONE_DEVICE (with an additional point for large pages).

> > Patch Layout
> > ============
> > The series is broken into two sections:
> > 
> > 1) N_MEMORY_PRIVATE Introduction.
> >    Introduce the node state.
> >    Opt those nodes out of mm/ services.

> > 2) NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_* features
> >    A set of mm/ service opt-in flags that augment private
> >    nodes to make them more useful (i.e. reclaim = overcommit).
> > 
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_LTPIN for private node folio pinning
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_NUMA_BALANCING for private-node NUMA balancing
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_DEMOTION for private-node tiering demotion
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_HOTUNPLUG for opted-in private nodes
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_USER_NUMA for userland numa controls
> >    NODE_PRIVATE_CAP_RECLAIM for opted-in private node reclaim
> >

> Looks reasonable, I wonder why USER_NUMA/HOTUNPUG is an opt-in?

Ideologically: Private nodes default to fully isolated, why should any
given feature be special?

Concretely: 

User-numa

   Some devices don't want the user to have control over placement.
   I have been working on compressed memory, for example, which only
   ever wants to be used as a reclaim-demotion target.
   
   (The reasoning for this is another thread, i plan on publishing
    my research on this this year)

Hot-unplug:

   Some devices can't necessarily handle unexpected migration, and
   hot-unplug is fundamentally a migration.  So the HOTUNPLUG cap
   actually means "hot-unplug can execute migrations".

   If the entire device has pre-drained the memory (all memory is free)
   then unplug works - it's just not very hot (no migrations) :]

   Maybe a naming issue?

~Gregory

On Wed, Jul 22, 2026 at 06:29:53PM +1000, Balbir Singh wrote:
> On 7/22/26 4:16 AM, Gregory Price wrote:
> > 
> > User-numa
> > 
> >    Some devices don't want the user to have control over placement.
> >    I have been working on compressed memory, for example, which only
> >    ever wants to be used as a reclaim-demotion target.

> >     my research on this this year)
> > 
> 
> I don't fully understand, how do we allocate memory on these devices then? Is
> the driver expected to allocate and map via vm_insert_page()?
>

It depends on your use case, but yes that's one possibility.

In another use case, you could enable (CAP_RECLAIM | CAP_DEMOTION) and
pages are allocated via alloc_demotion_target() in the reclaim path.

For general "device hosted memory", you can do a variety of things:

Direct alloc 
    ioctl(...) ->  explicit alloc call
    mmap(/dev/my_device) + mm_fault handler

Or a kernel-internal mempolicy
    ioctl(...) ->  set kernel-internal mempolicy on a vma
    mmap(/dev/my_device) + kernel-internal only mempolicy on the vma

The KVM guest_memfd patch included at the end of the series uses the
kernel-internal mempolicy mechanism:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/al-pkvmgIxGu3LzM@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/T/#me04a7e3556677babd82bfe3947b1a83b7f0017e1

This pattern is needed because the memory is never mapped into userland,
so userland actually *can't* set a mempolicy on this memory itself.

and then of course: CAP_USER_NUMA + mbind/set_mempolicy directly

This is kind of the point - the source of the memory has some control
over how it can be used.

> > Hot-unplug:
> > 
> >    Some devices can't necessarily handle unexpected migration, and
> >    hot-unplug is fundamentally a migration.  So the HOTUNPLUG cap
> >    actually means "hot-unplug can execute migrations".
> > 
> >    If the entire device has pre-drained the memory (all memory is free)
> >    then unplug works - it's just not very hot (no migrations) :]
> > 
> >    Maybe a naming issue?
> >

> Yes, I would prefer MIGRATION in the name, HOTUNPLUG made me wonder how these
> devices come online and because it is a device, it can go offline while the
> system is still online
> 

There's a difference between device hotplug and memory hotplug.  The
kernel's memory hotunplug system can at best be described as "best
effort", and if hotplugged as ZONE_NORMAL (on a non-private node) you're
unlikely to ever be able to hotunplug.

It's possible that this CAP bit should just go away and if the device
doesn't want its memory to be run migratable then it needs to hold extra
references on the folios.  That's probably reasonable and looks a lot
like a long term pin.

(note: I have to respin for Sashiko fixes, i'm likely going to drop most
of the CAP bits except USER_NUMA in v6 and have them come in with
specific use cases).

~Gregory

> Hi Gregory,
> 
> A question about this part, if my understanding is right,
> ZONELIST_PRIVATE contains all private nodes, should we use some explicit target
> nodemask here to make sure only the targeting owner's private node can be touched?
> 
> Otherwise it might fall back into a different owner's private node.

> 

Zonelists and the constructors here is a bit difficult to understand,
and I see I actually did a poor job of explaining what's going on in
the comments here.  I will fix that up in v6.

Lets say we have the following nodes:
   N_MEMORY :  0 1
   N_MEMORY_PRIVATE : 2 3

The following zonelists would exist:

ZONELIST_FALLBACK[0]   = {0,1}
ZONELIST_FALLBACK[1]   = {1,0}
ZONELIST_FALLBACK[2]   = {0,1}
ZONELIST_FALLBACK[3]   = {0,1}
ZONELIST_NOFALLBACK[0] = {0}
ZONELIST_NOFALLBACK[1] = {1}
ZONELIST_NOFALLBACK[2] = {}
ZONELIST_NOFALLBACK[3] = {}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[0]    = {0,1}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[1]    = {1,0}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[2]    = {2,0,1}  <- no fallback to 3
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[3]    = {3,0,1}  <- no fallback to 2
ZONELIST_PRIVATE_NOFALLBACK[0] = {0}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE_NOFALLBACK[1] = {1}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE_NOFALLBACK[2] = {2} <- __GFP_THISNODE
ZONELIST_PRIVATE_NOFALLBACK[3] = {3} <- __GFP_THISNODE

In the current design, we actually can never fallback to another private
node, and instead if you wanted to - for example - interleave across
said nodes you'd have to do so manually.

I think it's reasonable to later extend zonelist construction to support
such a think like:

ZONELIST_PRIVATE[2] = {2,0,1,3}

Later if such a need arises, but I would say wait for the use case.

~Gregory

review_comments.author: Gregory Price (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns about the design of Private Memory NUMA Nodes, specifically the use of a dedicated zonelist and the implications for global zonelist iteration and fallback allocations. The author explains that having multiple private nodes in each other's zonelists by default creates unclear implicit opt-ins and suggests isolating nodes in the zonelist instead. They acknowledge that this decision was intentional, but do not commit to revising the design.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jul 22, 2026 at 10:20:00PM +0800, Richard Cheng wrote:
> On Mon, Jul 20, 2026 at 03:33:54PM +0800, Gregory Price wrote:
> Hi Gregory,
> 
> I applied your series on mm-next and give a quick review,
> not thoroughly, still I have some questions below regarding the design.
>  

Hi Richard,

Thank you for the read.

Just a heads up, this was based on mm-new because of some recent work
on Brendan's page_alloc.h cleanup, but if you managed to get it applied
then maybe that's made its way forward already.

> > The goal here is to flip that dynamic, isolate by default and then
> > opt-in to specific services that the device says is safe.
> > 
> > Isolation at the NUMA/Zonelist layer provides a powerful mechanism for
> > memory hosted on accelerators - re-use of the kernel mm/ code.
> > 
> >  - Accelerators (GPUs) can use demotion, numactl, and reclaim.
> >  - Special memory devices (Compressed RAM) with special access controls
> >    (promote-on-write) can have generic services written for them.
> >  - Network devices with large memory regions intended for ring buffers
> >    can use the buddy and standard networking stack.
> >  - Slow, disaggregated memory pools which aren't suitable as general
> >    purpose memory get cleaner interfaces (no need to re-write the buddy
> >    in userland, can use migration interface, etc).
> >  - Per-workload dedicated memory nodes (disaggregated VM memory)
> > 
> 
> For accelerator part, take CXL Type-2 as example, it has its own protocol
> CXL.cache, CXL.mem which is the rule they need to obey during memory transaction,
> Adding more rules for them since they are NUMA node confused me, I wonder the reason ?
> 

CXL protocols simply state how to do the memory transaction at a
physical / transport level.  It does not make any statement on how the
operating systems are to make sense of these devices or what constructs
/ abstractions to build to actually manage the devices themselves.

This series is not necessarily attached to CXL in particular, you could
just as easily carve out memory from the general pool onto a private
node to ensure it only gets used for a particular use.

In fact, that is how I have been testing this with dax/kmem:
https://github.com/gourryinverse/linux/commit/f279e741d9c3643597525bfab916b29b95cb635b

> Because NUMA node, at least for me, representing topology/locality rather than
> something with ownership or capability or rules.
> 

The NUMA abstraction representing topology/locality is a construct we
(the OS developers) have decided on historically - but there's nothing
that dictates we can never create new useful abstractions with it.

Consider:
    N_NORMAL_MEMORY,        /* The node has regular memory */
    N_HIGH_MEMORY,          /* The node has regular or high memory */

These have nothing to do with topology or locality, they are node states
that only have meaning in the context of linux mm/.


To make something clear - there is no *requirement* for any particular
device to use this abstraction.  It simply enables a cleaner way for
devices carrying memory to re-utilize mm/ services while ensuring their
memory does not silently get used under system pressure (or some vagrant
in userspace doing `numactl --interleave all`).

Ignoring all the CAP bits entirely, if you just took the base series
your driver could re-use the buddy allocator without any special logic
AND have confidence that your driver is the only possible user of that
memory (barring some truly obscene bug).

> >  - isolation via a dedicated zonelist:
> >      - private nodes are omitted from FALLBACK/NOFALLBACK
> >      - added ZONELIST_PRIVATE(_NOFALLBACK)
> 
> I saw the reply in patch 5, so in fact there's not only one
> dedicate zonelist, but numerous ?
> I raise the question because zonelist was supposed to be a
> global, unbypasssable thing in MM design, but now what you
> are trying to do is to seperate the whole global list into several
> parts ?
> 
> Can you explain why in current design you don't consider to support
> something like ZONELIST_PRIVATE[n]={0, .. ,n-1} ? that's my imagination
> of what a global zonelist should look like, no matter private or non.
> 

First let me say that there's nothing that prevents us from doing this,
and we *could* make this the default case - but this decision was
intentional by me.

Having them all present in each-other's zonelists by default creates a
number of implicit opt-ins that are unclear:

  - any direct zonelist iterator now iterates all zones on all private
    nodes, even those nodes do not opt into the same services

    e.g. zonelist iteration in reclaim that targets Private Node A would
    attempt to reclaim Private Node B as well.  That would require and
    extra explicit filter.

    I ask: Why do this? Just isolate in the zonelist, and if there is
           a desire for intersections - make it explicit, not implicit.

  - fallback allocations can now occur across private nodes, even
    if those nodes are intended for different purposes.

    Obviously you can use nodemask to tighten the allocation target, but
    I use `numactl --interleave --all` as an example of a clear case
    where intersected nodelists may not give you the behavior you want.


So for consistency - everything including zonelists have full isolation.
This way all interactions with a private node must be explicit - always.


This is actually one of the problems with ZONE_DEVICE - and you can see
it in this patch set.  Some of the hooks in mm/ for zone_device only
apply to PTE cases, and are absent from PMD cases - only because PMD
mappings in ZONE_DEVICE aren't supported.

That kind of implicit behavior is quite bad.

That said, future improvements could include something like:

for_reclaimable_zone(ZONELIST_PRIVATE) {}

where we loosen this isolation, and formalize a filter, but I would
like to see the usecase for it first.  Loosening the isolation defeats
the entire purpose of the series, so there should be a strong reason to
do so.

> > Allocation Isolation
> > ====================
> > page_alloc presently controls whether a node's memory can be allocated
> > on a given call by 4 things (in order of authority)
> > 
> >   1) ZONELIST membership
> >      If a node is not in the walked zonelist, it's unreachable.
> >
> 
> So a device gets hotplugged in the system will get a dedicated zonelist here ?

Yes.

> And make sure it obeys the device's own protocol if it has one ?
> 

I'm not sure i follow this question, can you help me understand?

If by protocol you mean the CAP bits (opting into reclaim, demotion,
etc), then yes.  If you mean something else (CXL) then I think that's
orthogonal and unrelated.

> > Private nodes:
> >   1) Never appear in any ZONELIST_FALLBACK
> >   2) Have an empty ZONELIST_NOFALLBACK
> >   3) Only appear in their own ZONELIST_PRIVATE(_NOFALLBACK)
> > 
> > 1 & 2 mean all existing in-tree callers to page_alloc can NEVER
> > accidentally allocate from a private node.
> > 
> > An allocation must explicitly ask via a zonelist and a nodemask.
> > 
> >   alloc_flags |= ALLOC_ZONELIST_PRIVATE; /* use ZONELIST_PRIVATE */
> >   __alloc_pages(..., nodemask);     /* with the private node set */
> > 
> 
> As I stated above, NUMA concept was quite naive at first glance for my
> limited knowledge.
> 
> This is quite alot to add for NUMA node concept, I'll want to see
> more explanation in v6 and learn from it, thanks.
> 

Sure, I can expand on it.  I think it's not as much to add as you think
though - it's simply adding the concept of isolation to a NUMA node.

Some more explanation below, but if you think there is something i
should explicit spell out in v6 cover, please let me know.

---

I agree that NUMA as a concept was best-effort for, comically enough,
somewhat *Uniform* memory access - instead of *Non*-Uniform memory access.

The current abstraction quite nicely handles the case where all memory
on the system is roughly of the same calibre (DDR4, DDR5, etc) and
roughly for the same purpose (general system memory).

But it is quite incapable of handling truly heterogeneous memory systems
(precious HBM attached to the CPU, GPU's with HBM over a coherent link,
hardware-compressed memory expansion, network devices w/ memory, etc).

If you look at the history of ZONE_DEVICE, what it fundamentally does is
slaps an isolation mechanism on top of NUMA nodes because the NUMA
abstraction doesn't provide one. 

The problem with that approach is now you have to reason about a node
having both fungible and non-fungible memory.  It creates the need for
something like migrate_device.c when a properly isolated NUMA node could
just use migrate.c directly (with a coherent link).


One example of what isolation on the node enables:

A Private Node can hotplug memory in ZONE_NORMAL - which means it
can be GUP pinned.  That means driver support for GPU direct storage
is simply `alloc_pages_node() + pin()`. The driver doesn't have to
worry about something like SLAB accidentally using that same memory.

All without having to rewrite a bunch of mm/ in a driver, and with
having to do some kind of heroics with ZONE_DEVICE that causes even
more special mm/ interactions.

That only comes from adding an isolation primitive to a NUMA node.


> > Isolating private node folios from kernel services
> > ==================================================
> > We implement filter points in mm/ to prevent operations on
> > private node memory.  Where possible, we even re-use existing
> > filter points from ZONE_DEVICE.
> > 
> > Most filter points are one or two lines of code:
> > 
> > Combining ZONE_DEVICE and N_MEMORY_PRIVATE opt-out spots:
> >   -     if (folio_is_zone_device(folio))
> >   +     if (unlikely(folio_is_private_managed(folio)))
> > 
> > Disabling a service:
> >   +  if (!node_is_private(nid)) {
> >   +      kswapd_run(nid);
> >   +      kcompactd_run(nid);
> >   +  }
> > 
> > Disallowing a uapi interaction:
> >   +  if (node_state(nid, N_MEMORY_PRIVATE))
> >   +      return -EINVAL;
> > 
> 
> I'm not sure of why do we re-implement more filter and basically
> doing the same thing ? Any unavoidable scenario ?
> 
> re-using the exisintg filter would be nice if that's possible.
> 

We re-use (combine) existing filters where possible.

We add new ones where ZONE_DEVICE did not implement filters due to some
*implicit* filter already existing.

Two clear examples:
   - reclaim does not target ZONE_DEVICE
   - ZONE_DEVICE does not support PMD

Both cases result in private-node filters that otherwise would have to
exist for ZONE_DEVICE (and if ZONE_DEVICE ever grows PMD support, those
filters will have to be added).

> > Bonus Configuration: HBM device memory tiering
> > ==============================================
> > echo 1 > dax0.0/private       # make the node private
> > echo 0 > dax0.0/adistance     # highest tier
> > echo 1 > dax0.0/reclaim       # reclaim active
> > echo 1 > dax0.0/demotion      # may demote from the node
> > echo 1 > dax0.0/user_numa     # mbind()
> > echo online_movable > dax0.0/state
> > echo 1 > numa/demotion_enabled
> > 
> > This is an HBM device which is treated as the top-tier in the
> > system but for which memory can only enter via explicit mbind().
> > 
> > It can be overcommitted because it can be reclaimed (demotions
> > go to CPU DRAM, and reclaim can swap from it).
> > 
> > If the HBM is managed by an accelerator (GPU), the mmu_notifier
> > allows it to know when reclaim is moving memory out to do
> > device-mmu invalidation prior to migration.
> > 
> > Prereqs, base commit, references
> > ================================
> > akpm/mm-new - for Brendan Jackman's mm/page_alloc.h work[3]
> > 
> 
> Still thanks for the work, I learn alot from your work as well, thanks.
> 

Of course, and thank you for reading.

If nothing else I hope this series helps folks understand the page
allocator better (it certainly has helped me).  If there is anything
you think I can improve or better explain, I am happy to discuss.

I am planning a larger publication of all my research sometime in the
future, but I think code is more impactful and useful - so I am
prioritizing that for now :].

~Gregory

---

On Wed, Jul 22, 2026 at 11:40:06AM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> On Wed, Jul 22, 2026 at 10:20:00PM +0800, Richard Cheng wrote:
> > 
> > I saw the reply in patch 5, so in fact there's not only one
> > dedicate zonelist, but numerous ?
> > I raise the question because zonelist was supposed to be a
> > global, unbypasssable thing in MM design, but now what you
> > are trying to do is to seperate the whole global list into several
> > parts ?
> > 
> > Can you explain why in current design you don't consider to support
> > something like ZONELIST_PRIVATE[n]={0, .. ,n-1} ? that's my imagination
> > of what a global zonelist should look like, no matter private or non.
> > 
> 
> First let me say that there's nothing that prevents us from doing this,
> and we *could* make this the default case - but this decision was
> intentional by me.
> 

You caught me on something here - I am wrong about my own code.

I described to you how I originally implemented zonelist isolation,
which I walked back when I was testing mempolicy and demotion.

The current code and the comments in the code are correct, we get
the following zonelists.

N_MEMORY : 0,1
N_MEMORY_PRIVATE: 2,3

ZONELIST_PRIVATE[0]     = {0,1,2,3}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[1]     = {1,0,2,3}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[2]     = {2,0,1,3}
ZONELIST_PRIVATE[3]     = {3,0,1,2}

Meaning everything except for MPOL_F_RELATIVE_NODES works (because
of cpuset isolation, but more on this in a moment).


So what I shipped here is what you are describing.

Looking back at my notes - I realized a few things that lead me to
walk back that isolation:

1) mempolicy makes more sense this way.
2) reclaim/demotion is easier to implement without isolation.
3) The individual service checks and nodelists handle the rest.
4) It overally just makes more sense.  If something breaks isolation
   it's because there's a bug, not because of a structural issue.

For example in demotion we do this:

demote_folio_list():
    /* Get demotion targets, find the best one, and demote */
    node_get_allowed_targets(pgdat, &allowed_mask);
    target_nid = next_demotion_node(pgdat->node_id, &allowed_mask);
    migrate_pages(demote_folios, alloc_demote_folio, ...);

Where the first step already filters on CAP_DEMOTION.
The result is still clean - no special iterators needed.

What I realized from your questions here is that since i walked back the
zonelist isolation, I think i can actually include any CAP_USER_NUMA
node in cpuset.mems and allow parititioning fairly trivially.

I think this might be the last majority complexity falling out.

Thank you for the questions - this has helped.

~Gregory

---

On Wed, Jul 22, 2026 at 05:19:42PM -0700, SJ Park wrote:
> On Wed, 22 Jul 2026 08:16:56 -0400 Gregory Price <gourry@gourry.net> wrote:

I have been very much discouraged from this kind of trimming because it
removes the context of the series from the individual patches - making it

But I understand, I will try to remember to do this for you.

> So, I understand later patches will make it optionally reclaimable and update
> this restriction by the 26th patch?  That sounds fair.  But, could we drop "by
> default" from the above comment for reducing the confusion?

Yes, but you bring up a good point - I think this two-step process is
actually poor form and that I need to redo it.

Maybe this can be redone as node states (features?) such as:

   N_MEMORY_RECLAIMABLE
   N_MEMORY_DEMOTABLE
   N_MEMORY_TIERABLE

so these damon changes will forego this confusing intermediate step of:

   if (folio_is_private_node(folio))

and go straight to

   if (node_is_reclaimable(folio_nid(folio)))

In the first patch - without ever having to revisit damon again.

> > 
> > Technically there is nothing in migration core to prevent migration
> > operations, it's done on a service basis - hotunplug, reclaim/demotion,
> > user numa (mbind, migrate/move_pages) etc.
> > 
> > Operations on private nodes/private node folioes are refused if the
> > capability bit is not set.
> 
> I haven't had a chance to read the entire series, sorry about that.  So, do you
> mean the above blocking is not really needed, or that will conditionally be

I will reduce this all to a single patch in v6, I see where things can

Thanks SJ!
